title: Coppice hazel bean poles
subtitle: Harvesting and preparing hazel rods for runner bean wigwams
excerpt: Hazel bean poles from a managed coppice stool: cut in winter, de-branched, bark-stripped, pointed at one end, and dried before spring planting. A single hazel stool cut on rotation produces enough rods for a kitchen garden indefinitely. This guide covers selection, harvesting, preparation, and the wigwam configuration.
sourceNotes: Traditional British kitchen-garden coppice management; Cassell's Household Guide (1880) vol. I for bean-rod preparation and traditional wigwam dimensions.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently cut timber, still high in moisture content. Hazel bean poles are harvested green and driven into the ground before they dry fully.
The practice of cutting deciduous trees at the base to encourage vigorous multi-stem re-growth. The harvested rods are called coppice poles or withies.
In this context, a rod before it has been pointed and de-branched; the rough material that will become the finished pole.

content.text: A hazel
coppice

stool cut on a 7 to 10 year rotation produces 20 to 30 straight rods per cut; enough for a full kitchen garden's bean poles with some left for pea sticks and hurdle stakes. Hazel rods resist soil decay better than most softwoods, last 3 to 5 years in the ground, and are available free to anyone with a managed woodland or a corner of garden with an established hazel. This guide covers the winter harvest, the spring preparation, and the wigwam setup.
The wood

Select straight rods from the previous year's coppice growth: 2 to 2.5 cm in diameter at the base, 220 to 250 cm long. Rods should be free of significant side branches over their working length (the bottom 200 cm); a few small branches near the top are left on to provide support for the growing beans to grip.
Green wood
hazel harvested in winter (November to February) when the sap is down is strongest. Summer-cut rods have more moisture and are more prone to splitting at the point.
Tools

A Yorkshire billhook for harvesting and de-branching; the heavy blade and curved tip make short work of hazel stems up to 3 cm in one stroke. A sloyd knife for pointing. A leather strop. No other tools are needed for basic bean poles; a pruning saw for stems over 3 cm at the base.
Method
Prepare each
blank
rod in sequence: harvest, de-branch, point, bundle. Work from a kneeling or low-seated position for the billhook cuts; standing cuts on thin rods lose control.

Cut the rod at the base with a single angled billhook stroke, leaving a clean slanted cut rather than a ragged one. A slanted base cut sheds rainwater rather than holding it, which extends the coppice stool's life. Aim 2 to 3 cm above the previous cut's scar.

De-branch the lower 200 cm with the billhook. Hold the rod against your leg (never across the lap) and strip the side branches downward with short billhook blows. Leave the upper 30 to 50 cm branched; the beans will wind through the upper twigs for support.

Point the base end with the sloyd knife: four or six facets tapering to a blunt point over the bottom 8 cm. The point should be blunt, not needle-sharp; a sharp point breaks on stony soil, while a blunt point drives more cleanly. Make push cuts along the grain from the tip upward.

Bundle the finished poles loosely in groups of 8 to 10 and store upright or horizontally under cover for 4 weeks before use. Driving a fully green pole into soil in the first week of cutting sometimes causes the tip to split as the wood swells from soil moisture contact.

Set up the wigwam: drive 8 poles in a circle 60 cm in diameter, each pole at a slight inward angle. Tie the tops together 30 cm from the tips with twine in a simple lashing. A well-set wigwam will hold 10 to 12 runner bean plants and withstand summer wind without staking.
Finishing
No finish required. Bean poles are outdoor seasonal tools; any oil treatment on the surface reduces the grip that bean plants rely on to wind around the pole.
Variations
Pea sticks: shorter, branchier rods 80 to 100 cm long, left fully branched. Pushed into the ground at an angle to form a network of twigs that peas can scramble through.
Tent ridge: two wigwams connected by a horizontal ridge pole lashed between the tops, producing a tent shape. Provides more planting area than the round wigwam.
Ash poles: longer-lived than hazel in the ground (5 to 7 years) but less flexible and harder to point cleanly. Use ash if hazel is not available locally.
Care

Pull the poles from the soil at season end, scrape off the soil, and store dry under cover. Poles driven into the ground while still green will last 3 to 4 seasons if stored dry between uses. Replace individual poles when they snap at the base.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems with coppice hazel bean poles.

items.symptom: Point splits on driving into hard ground
items.cause: Point too sharp, or wood still very green when driven
items.fix: Keep the point blunt; 15 mm across the tip. If the soil is hard, pre-pierce the hole with a steel dibber before driving the pole.

items.cause: Poles not angled inward enough, or top lashing too low
items.fix: Each pole should lean inward by 10 to 15 degrees. Tie the tops as close to the pole ends as possible; a lashing low on the poles acts as a pivot point rather than an anchor.

items.symptom: Poles decay at the soil line after one season
items.cause: Poles left in wet soil over winter without lifting
items.fix: Pull poles at the end of the growing season and store dry. Poles left in wet soil through winter decay at the buried section regardless of species.
